title: C4 rotation-vote field: average field logits over the crop's four 90° turns
hypothesis: The frozen eval set is heading-agnostic (dataset.py gives every held-out crop a deterministic random rotation), but train.py's training tensor shows each training location at exactly ONE random heading, frozen for all 8 epochs — so at eval the model must recognize places across up to 180° of heading mismatch with a plain CNN + FC field head that has no rotational structure whatsoever. Invariance-by-data was tried once (exp 6, fresh rotations every epoch) and refuted: making a 1024-way memorization head learn every location at every heading is sample-inefficient, and more rotated examples did not help. The untried alternative is invariance by construction: evaluate the field on the crop's four exact 90° pixel rotations and average the logits (C4 symmetrization). This (a) guarantees one of the four evaluated views lies within 45° of the one trained heading, versus up to 180° today; (b) makes the model exactly invariant over the C4 subgroup, so training capacity only has to cover a ±45° residual; and (c) since a logit mean is a geometric-mean ensemble, a lookalike cell that fires at only one heading is multiplicatively suppressed — directly attacking the committed-wrong 2–4 km error mode that exps 14/15 diagnosed as what pins every cell's median near 1 km.
method: model/model.py forward() only: stack the input with its three 90° pixel rotations (transpose+flip — exact permutations, no interpolation, ONNX-safe) into a 4N batch, run the unchanged trunk + layout squeeze + lighting gate + two-expert heads on all four copies, average the four 1024-d field logit vectors (and the four GAP descriptors for the confidence head's input) before the unchanged β-sharpened decode and field-shape confidence. loss_fn, train.py, calibration, and the ONNX export contract untouched.
expected_outcome: A broad drop across all 24 area×bucket cells rather than a single-cell fix: worst-case median (currently Munich night, 1368.6 m) improves ≥8% to ≤ ~1250 m if heading mismatch is a binding error source; roughly flat (±3%) if it is not. Model bytes unchanged (~3.3 MiB vs 4 MiB gate); host latency proxy ~4× (≈3 ms vs 250 ms gate); training wall time ~4× (~70 min/area) with no train.py change.
eli5: A drone photo can be taken facing any direction, but during practice the model only ever saw each place from one direction — so at test time it often fails to recognize a spot simply because the picture is turned. The fix: show the model every photo four times — spun by 0°, 90°, 180° and 270° — and let the four looks vote on where the drone is. A wrong guess that only appears at one spin gets outvoted; the right answer keeps winning at every spin. Same brain, four glances, one vote.

stages.name: Camera frame
stages.detail: 128×128 px aerial crop, one of 6 simulated lighting conditions

stages.name: Rotation fan
stages.detail: the crop is copied into its four 90° turns (exact pixel re-arrangements, no blur) and all four copies travel through the same network side by side

stages.name: Feature extractor
stages.detail: ImageNet-pretrained MobileNetV3-Small (first 9 blocks, 190k params), gently fine-tuned

stages.name: Layout summary
stages.detail: a slim extra layer condenses the 8×8 feature grid into a 512-number code that remembers what sits where in the crop; the texture average (48 numbers) is kept alongside

stages.name: Lighting gate
stages.detail: a tiny network reads the crop's own average brightness together with the 48-number texture summary and outputs one number: how night-like is this shot?

stages.name: Probability map
stages.detail: two scorers read each of the four turned copies and the gate blends their per-cell guesses as before; the four resulting heat maps are then averaged into one voted map — a location only stays hot if it wins from every direction

stages.name: Decode
stages.detail: the heat map's contrast is turned up first (peaks boosted, faint background suppressed), then the answer is the balance point of the sharpened map — committing to the strongest hotspot instead of averaging every faint guess across the whole city

stages.name: Confidence
stages.detail: the model now inspects the shape of its own heat map — one crisp hotspot means sure, a washed-out smear means unsure — and below a tuned bar it says 'no fix' instead of guessing; the bar is set so it still answers at least 40% of the time in every lighting condition

stages.name: Training signal
stages.detail: unchanged — same position and confidence grading as before; because the four-way vote lives inside the model, training grades exactly the voted answer that flies

implementation_brief:
Edit model/model.py ONLY. model/train.py must NOT change in any way — its CLI (--area, --out-dir, --data-dir, --epochs, --max-crops-per-bucket, --seed), the two-LR param groups, the conf_shift calibration flow, and the export call all stay byte-identical. Frozen contracts to preserve (restated from model/model.py's docstring): train.py exports one ONNX per area at <out>/models/<area>.onnx taking a 1x3x128x128 float32 input named 'frame' in [0,1] and returning output 'uvc' = [[u, v, conf]] with u, v normalized map coords and conf in [0,1]; export via the existing export_onnx (opset 17, dynamo=False); exported file must stay <= 4 MiB (this change adds zero parameters, so it will); the frozen scorer's host-latency proxy gate is 250 ms (4x of today's ~0.8 ms is fine). Do not touch GRID_K, TARGET_SIGMA_CELLS, DECODE_BETA, GOOD_ERR_UV, loss_fn, export_onnx, estimate_position, __init__ (no new parameters or buffers), or any loss weights.

1) Add a module-level helper above TinyLocNet:
   def _c4_stack(x):
       """[N,3,H,W] -> [4N,3,H,W]: x with its three 90-degree pixel rotations
       (exact permutations via transpose+flip; rotation-major block order)."""
       r90 = x.transpose(2, 3).flip(2)
       r180 = x.flip(2).flip(3)
       r270 = x.transpose(2, 3).flip(3)
       return torch.cat([x, r90, r180, r270], dim=0)
   (Use transpose+flip, NOT torch.rot90, so the torchscript ONNX exporter maps to Transpose + Slice ops it definitely supports.)

2) Rewrite TinyLocNet.forward keeping its exact signature (self, x, return_logits: bool = False) and return contract (out, or (out, logits) where logits is [N, grid_k*grid_k] — loss_fn consumes it unchanged):
   n = x.shape[0]
   lum = x.mean(dim=(1, 2, 3)).unsqueeze(1)          # rotation-invariant, from x only
   x4 = _c4_stack(x)
   fmap = self.features((x4 - self.norm_mean) / self.norm_std)   # buffers broadcast over 4N
   f4 = fmap.mean(dim=(2, 3))                        # [4N,48]
   layout4 = self.layout_squeeze(fmap).flatten(1)    # [4N,512]
   lum4 = lum.repeat(4, 1)                           # [4N,1]
   bright4 = self.loc_logits(torch.cat([f4, layout4], dim=1))
   dark4 = self.dark_logits(f4)
   g4 = torch.sigmoid(self.gate(torch.cat([lum4, f4], dim=1)))
   logits4 = (1 - g4) * bright4 + g4 * dark4         # [4N, K*K] per-turn gated blend
   logits = logits4.reshape(4, n, -1).mean(dim=0)    # C4 logit average (geometric-mean vote)
   f = f4.reshape(4, n, -1).mean(dim=0)              # averaged GAP for the conf head
   ...then continue VERBATIM with the current tail: p = softmax(DECODE_BETA*logits), u, v soft-argmax; p1 = softmax(logits), u1, v1; pd/peak/ent/gap conf features; z = conf_head(cat([f.detach(), peak, ent, gap])); conf = sigmoid(z - conf_shift); out = cat([u, v, conf]); return (out, logits) if return_logits else out.
   The reshape(4, n, -1) is correct because _c4_stack concatenates rotation-major blocks along dim 0. The regression target (crop center) is rotation-invariant, so averaging is target-consistent and loss_fn needs no change. Gradients flow through all four branches — the symmetrized ensemble is what trains AND what flies.

3) Add a short 'Exp 16:' paragraph to the module docstring explaining the C4 rotation-vote (eval is heading-agnostic, training showed each location at one frozen heading; averaging logits over the four exact 90° turns gives built-in C4 invariance and outvotes single-heading lookalike misfires).

4) Smoke test before finishing (no training):
   .venv/bin/python - <<'EOF'
   import torch, numpy as np
   from model.model import build_model, export_onnx
   m = build_model().eval()
   x = torch.rand(2, 3, 128, 128)
   with torch.no_grad():
       out = m(x); o2 = m(x.transpose(2, 3).flip(2))
   assert out.shape == (2, 3) and torch.isfinite(out).all()
   assert torch.allclose(out[:, :2], o2[:, :2], atol=1e-4)  # exact C4 invariance
   export_onnx(m, '/tmp/exp16.onnx')
   import onnxruntime as ort
   s = ort.InferenceSession('/tmp/exp16.onnx', providers=['CPUExecutionProvider'])
   y = s.run(None, {'frame': np.random.rand(1, 3, 128, 128).astype(np.float32)})[0]
   assert y.shape == (1, 3) and np.isfinite(y).all() and 0 <= y[0, 2] <= 1
   import os; assert os.path.getsize('/tmp/exp16.onnx') <= 4 * 1024 * 1024
   print('smoke OK', y)
   EOF
   The invariance assert is the key regression check: rotating the input permutes the internal 4-stack, so (u, v) must be identical up to float associativity. If the ONNX exporter rejects flip/transpose (it should not at opset 17), fall back to torch.rot90(x, k, dims=(2, 3)) for k=1,2,3 and re-run the smoke test.

5) Compute notes, no action needed: effective trunk batch becomes 4x64=256 at 128², fine for MobileNetV3-Small on the run GPU; training wall time rises ~4x (~70 min/area, areas run in parallel, no harness timeout). Do NOT shrink the batch size, epochs, or crop counts to compensate — train.py stays untouched.
